content.text: The Fairness Criteria Landscape

A fairness metric is a mathematical criterion that specifies what 'equal treatment' means for a predictive system. Each criterion captures a different moral intuition about equality. The critical insight, established by Chouldechova (2017) and Kleinberg et al. (2016), is that most pairs of fairness criteria are
mathematically incompatible
when the positive outcome base rate differs between groups — which it almost always does in practice. Choosing a fairness criterion is therefore an ethical and policy decision, not a technical one.

attrs.alt: Three fairness criteria (Demographic Parity, Equalized Odds, Calibration) with incompatibility warnings between them

content.text: Demographic Parity

Demographic parity (also called statistical parity) requires that the proportion of individuals receiving a positive outcome be equal across groups. It makes no reference to ground truth labels — only to the model's predictions:

attrs.latex: P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=a) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid A=b) \quad \text{for all groups } a, b

content.text:
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class FairnessResult:
    metric: str
    group_a: str
    group_b: str
    value_a: float
    value_b: float
    difference: float           # |value_a - value_b|
    passes: bool                # True if within tolerance

def demographic_parity(
    predictions: dict[str, list[int]],   # group -> list of 0/1 predictions
    tolerance: float = 0.05,
) -> list[FairnessResult]:
    """
    Check demographic parity across all pairs of groups.
    tolerance: maximum allowed difference in positive prediction rates.
    """
    rates = {g: sum(preds) / len(preds) for g, preds in predictions.items() if preds}
    groups = sorted(rates.keys())
    results = []
    for i, a in enumerate(groups):
        for b in groups[i+1:]:
            diff = abs(rates[a] - rates[b])
            results.append(FairnessResult(
                metric="demographic_parity",
                group_a=a,
                group_b=b,
                value_a=round(rates[a], 4),
                value_b=round(rates[b], 4),
                difference=round(diff, 4),
                passes=diff <= tolerance,
            ))
    return results

# LLM résumé scoring: fraction of résumés rated "strong" per group
predictions = {
    "group_majority": [1]*70 + [0]*30,   # 70 % positive rate
    "group_minority": [1]*50 + [0]*50,   # 50 % positive rate
}
for r in demographic_parity(predictions, tolerance=0.05):
    status = "PASS" if r.passes else "FAIL"
    print(f"  [{status}] {r.group_a} vs {r.group_b}: "
          f"{r.value_a:.0%} vs {r.value_b:.0%}, diff={r.difference:.2f}")
# [FAIL] group_majority vs group_minority: 70% vs 50%, diff=0.20
Equal Opportunity
Equal opportunity (Hardt et al., 2016) requires only that the
true positive rate

(TPR, also called recall or sensitivity) be equal across groups. It focuses exclusively on beneficial outcomes: all qualified individuals should have an equal chance of being correctly identified as qualified, regardless of group membership:

attrs.latex: P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, A=a) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=1, A=b)

content.text: Equalized Odds
Equalized odds strengthens equal opportunity by requiring both the true positive rate and the
false positive rate
(FPR) to be equal across groups. It constrains both types of errors simultaneously — no group should bear a disproportionate share of either false rejections or false acceptances:

attrs.latex: P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=y, A=a) = P(\hat{Y}=1 \mid Y=y, A=b) \quad \text{for } y \in \{0, 1\}

content.text:
def equalized_odds(
    predictions: dict[str, list[int]],
    ground_truth: dict[str, list[int]],
    tolerance: float = 0.05,
) -> list[FairnessResult]:
    """
    Returns two FairnessResult per pair: one for TPR, one for FPR.
    Equalized odds is satisfied only if BOTH pass.
    """
    def rates(preds: list[int], labels: list[int]) -> tuple[float, float]:
        pos = [(p, y) for p, y in zip(preds, labels) if y == 1]
        neg = [(p, y) for p, y in zip(preds, labels) if y == 0]
        tpr = sum(p for p, _ in pos) / len(pos) if pos else float("nan")
        fpr = sum(p for p, _ in neg) / len(neg) if neg else float("nan")
        return tpr, fpr

    group_rates = {g: rates(predictions[g], ground_truth[g]) for g in predictions}
    groups = sorted(group_rates.keys())
    results = []
    for i, a in enumerate(groups):
        for b in groups[i+1:]:
            for rate_idx, rate_name in [(0, "TPR"), (1, "FPR")]:
                va, vb = group_rates[a][rate_idx], group_rates[b][rate_idx]
                diff = abs(va - vb)
                results.append(FairnessResult(
                    metric=f"equalized_odds_{rate_name}",
                    group_a=a,
                    group_b=b,
                    value_a=round(va, 4),
                    value_b=round(vb, 4),
                    difference=round(diff, 4),
                    passes=diff <= tolerance,
                ))
    return results
Calibration

Calibration requires that the model's predicted probability score have the same meaning for all groups. A well-calibrated model satisfies: among all individuals scored at 70 %, exactly 70 % are truly positive — and this holds equally for every demographic group. Poorly calibrated models assign the same score but different implied probabilities to members of different groups:
The Impossibility Result
Chouldechova (2017) proved that when the base rates differ between groups, you cannot simultaneously achieve calibration, equal opportunity, and equalized false positive rates. Concretely: if
, then satisfying calibration forces the TPR and FPR to differ between groups, and vice versa. This is not an engineering limitation — it is a mathematical constraint.
The practical consequence is that choosing a fairness criterion is a normative decision about which errors matter most in a given context:
Hiring / lending: demographic parity
— if the goal is equal allocation of opportunities, parity in selection rates is the right anchor even if it ignores base-rate differences.
Medical screening: equal opportunity
— missing a positive case (false negative) is the primary harm; require equal recall across groups while accepting that false positive rates may differ.
Criminal risk scoring: equalized odds
— both types of error carry serious harm (wrongful detention vs. missed intervention); constrain both TPR and FPR equally.
Credit scoring (communicated to applicants): calibration
— a score communicated to humans must mean the same thing for everyone; otherwise the score is dishonest.
LLM-Specific Fairness Metrics
LLMs require fairness metrics that go beyond binary classification. Three domain-specific metrics are most widely used:

from typing import Callable

# 1. Toxicity gap
def toxicity_gap(
    prompts_by_group: dict[str, list[str]],
    generate_fn: Callable[[str], str],
    toxicity_score_fn: Callable[[str], float],  # returns 0.0–1.0
) -> dict[str, float]:
    """
    Average toxicity of model outputs, broken down by prompt group.
    A gap > 0.05 between groups flags a fairness issue.
    """
    group_scores = {}
    for group, prompts in prompts_by_group.items():
        outputs = [generate_fn(p) for p in prompts]
        scores = [toxicity_score_fn(o) for o in outputs]
        group_scores[group] = sum(scores) / len(scores)
    return group_scores


# 2. Regard score gap (Sheng et al., 2019)
# Regard measures sentiment/attitude toward a demographic group.
# A gap in regard scores across groups indicates stereotyped output.
def regard_gap(
    templates: list[str],             # e.g. "The {group} person is known for ..."
    group_names: list[str],           # e.g. ["man", "woman", "non-binary person"]
    generate_fn: Callable[[str], str],
    regard_score_fn: Callable[[str], float],  # positive sentiment → +1; negative → -1
) -> dict[str, float]:
    scores = {}
    for group in group_names:
        outputs = [generate_fn(t.format(group=group)) for t in templates]
        scores[group] = sum(regard_score_fn(o) for o in outputs) / len(outputs)
    return scores


# 3. Quality gap (per-group ROUGE / BLEU)
def quality_gap(
    reference_pairs: dict[str, list[tuple[str, str]]],  # group -> [(prompt, reference)]
    generate_fn: Callable[[str], str],
    score_fn: Callable[[str, str], float],   # (generated, reference) -> score
) -> dict[str, float]:
    scores = {}
    for group, pairs in reference_pairs.items():
        group_scores = [score_fn(generate_fn(p), ref) for p, ref in pairs]
        scores[group] = sum(group_scores) / len(group_scores)
    return scores
Metric Selection Guide
Scenario
Recommended Metric
Why
LLM-based résumé screening
Demographic parity + equal opportunity
Resource allocation + equal recall for qualified candidates
Medical triage assistant
Equal opportunity (TPR parity)
Missing a true positive is the dominant harm; FPR asymmetry is acceptable
Recidivism risk tool
Equalized odds
Both false detentions and missed risks harm individuals
Credit risk score shown to applicants
Calibration by group
Score must be interpretable and honest across groups
Open-ended LLM (chatbot)
Toxicity gap + regard gap
No ground truth; measure quality and sentiment differentials across demographic prompts
LLM translation tool
Quality gap (per-language BLEU)
Detect languages where output quality is systematically lower
Summary

Fairness metrics formalise different ethical intuitions about equal treatment. Demographic parity requires equal selection rates; equal opportunity requires equal recall; equalized odds constrains both error types; calibration requires consistent score meaning. The Chouldechova impossibility result means that when base rates differ across groups — which is typical — satisfying all criteria simultaneously is mathematically impossible. Choosing a metric is therefore a normative decision guided by which error type carries the most harm in the specific deployment context. For LLMs, domain-specific metrics (toxicity gap, regard gap, quality gap) extend the classical binary-classification criteria to open-ended generation settings.

A fairness metric is not a technical fact about your model. It is a statement about which kind of injustice your deployment context most needs to avoid — and that statement belongs to policy, not engineering.
